summary: Gruber reflects on the final Macworld Expo with Apple's participation, framing the piece around The Tempest, a timeless San Francisco dive bar near the Moscone Center. He surveys the show floor, finding it healthy but uncertain, with exhibitors wanting to return but waiting to see who else commits. He argues that Apple's rationale for leaving — that Apple Stores reach enough customers — misses the point: the Expo uniquely brings together designers, engineers, press, and users in irreplaceable face-to-face encounters. He concludes that while Apple may not need the Expo, the broader Mac community does, and its potential loss would leave a void nothing else can fill.
key_points: The health of Macworld Expo was traditionally judged by the North Hall — this year it looked good, if not great, but uncertainty loomed over the show's future without Apple
Exhibitors overwhelmingly wanted Macworld to continue but were caught in a chicken-and-egg problem, each waiting to see who else would commit before signing on
Apple's argument that Apple Stores replace the Expo ignores that 90% of exhibited products aren't in Apple Stores, and that meeting customers and peers in person has irreplaceable value
The symbiotic relationship between large polished exhibitors and small scrappy booths creates the cocktail that makes a healthy trade show — Apple's absence threatens that balance
As the world goes more digital, real-world interactions become more valuable, not less
money_quotes: At some bars you have a drink; at The Tempest you just drink.
The Tempest is exactly the bar it wants to be.
Come next January, The Tempest will remain unchanged. Macworld Expo will not.
You no longer need to be in the same place to forge a community or to gain a customer.
As the world goes ever more digital, the remaining real-world interactions become more valuable.
If the only purpose of Macworld Expo, from Apple's perspective, was to showcase the company's own latest products, then they're right, they no longer have any need for it. But the rest of us do.
There is nothing else like Macworld Expo, and if it fades away, there will be nothing to take its place.
I suggest a corollary: that the truth often resists being written down or transmitted over a wire, and will only be told to your face.
key_insight: Apple's withdrawal from Macworld Expo may serve the company's interests, but it threatens an irreplaceable community institution where face-to-face connection delivers something digital channels cannot.
